summary: Buddhism has been an important part of my life, though I couldn't attend worship services often when my children were little. Recently, I have been attending constantly and feel undeservedly blessed when I listen to the words. I believe religion, including Buddhism, is essential for children to develop a good heart and avoid bad behavior. It has given me strength through difficult times, especially during and after the war.

Priest Ishimoki once visited me and kindly wrote in English on my trunk 'Keep this box' to prevent it from being burned. I had originally written 'Don't burn this' in Japanese because I couldn't write English, although I could read it. His visit was helpful in preserving my family records, showing his thoughtful support during a difficult time.

Rev. Takarabe visited me with much trouble, and I was encouraged to join and talk with them. His visit was significant enough that I was invited to participate in discussions, reflecting the community connections we maintained. It showed the effort religious leaders made to keep us connected despite hardships.

I came from Japan, where I was sent at age six for schooling, thinking education there was better for my children. I returned to the United States during the Hoover Depression at age eighteen after graduating high school in Japan. Visiting Japan again later, especially when my father was ill, was a happy and memorable experience. Japan’s traditions and culture are something I believe we must preserve even while living in America.

I came back from Japan during the Hoover Depression, which was a very difficult time economically. I was eighteen years old then, and it was the worst time for many people, including our community. Returning during such hardship made life challenging but also shaped my understanding of resilience.

The Chronicle, Hearst's newspaper in San Francisco, was really bad to us Japanese during the exclusionary movement. They contributed to the negative mood against us, especially before and during World War II. Their coverage made life harder for Japanese Americans trying to live peacefully in the United States.

Hearst Corporation owned The Chronicle, which was very hostile toward Japanese Americans during the exclusionary period. Their newspapers spread negative sentiments that affected our community deeply. This made it harder for us to be accepted and find employment before the war.

World War II was a racial war, and it was very bad for Japanese people when Japan lost. However, after the war, many Japanese Americans found employment opportunities they had never had before, especially in government jobs. The war changed our lives drastically, both through hardship and eventual progress.

I was born and raised in the United States, though I spent many years in Japan for schooling. Coming back here during the Hoover Depression was a shock because America was not as developed as I expected. I have always felt proud to be Japanese but also recognize the challenges of living in America, including discrimination and the need to work hard.

My passport was issued in Kobe, Japan, in 1918 when I was preparing to return to the United States with my daughter. Kobe was the place where official documents were processed for travel back to America. It marks an important point in my life when I was transitioning between the two countries.

I am Mitsuyo Sakada, born in Japan and later living in the United States. I traveled back and forth between Japan and America, especially during difficult times like when my father was ill. I have raised my children here and tried to preserve our Japanese heritage while adapting to life in America.

Sumiko Sakada is my daughter, born in Oakland, California in 1916. She traveled with me back to Japan when we received news about my father's critical condition. She often visits me here, and I am proud of her as part of our family’s story bridging Japan and America.

Oakland, California is where my daughter Sumiko was born in 1916. It is part of our family’s American roots, even though we spent time in Japan for schooling and family matters. Oakland represents the beginning of our American life before the challenges of the Depression and war.

Traveling by boat to Yokohama from the United States took only two days when I returned to Japan with my daughters. I remember throwing away my daughter's diapers into the sea during the trip. Yokohama was the port where we arrived, marking the start of our visit to Japan after many years.

Yoshiko Oshima is my eldest daughter who was already married when we traveled back to Japan together. She accompanied me and Sumiko when we went to visit my father during his illness. Yoshiko has been an important part of our family, bridging generations and countries.

My daughter Kimiko married into the Miyamoto family. This connection represents how our family grew and integrated into the Japanese American community. The Miyamotos are part of our extended family history.

Mrs. Ai Miyasaki is a neighbor who stayed in a hotel in Fresno after leaving the internment camp before moving back to Sacramento. We have shared stories about our experiences during and after the war. She is part of the community that helped each other through difficult times.

Fresno was where Mrs. Ai Miyasaki stayed in a hotel after leaving the internment camp before returning to Sacramento. It was part of the post-war resettlement for many Japanese Americans. Fresno represents a transitional place in our community’s history.

Sacramento is where my husband was born and where we decided to settle after the war. Although we considered moving back to Japan, we ultimately stayed in America because my husband did not know Japanese well. Sacramento became our home and the place where our family rebuilt our lives.

There was a commanding officer named Webster during the war who questioned my husband about his allegiance. My husband, a true-born Nisei, initially answered 'no, no' but later changed to 'yes' to show his loyalty to America despite not wanting to serve. This interaction was tense but also showed the difficult position many Japanese Americans faced.

My husband was a true-born Nisei who could not write Japanese because he was raised here. The Nisei generation faced many challenges, including language barriers and loyalty tests during the war. Their experiences were different from the Issei, and I see the importance of remembering their struggles.

My husband was sent to Gerome after his allegiance interview during the war. It was one of the places where Japanese Americans were relocated. Gerome represents part of the difficult wartime experience for our family and community.

Sansei, the third generation, often do not know much about the Issei’s culture or hardships. I wish they would be proud of being Japanese and understand our traditions. I believe it is important for Sansei to remember where they come from and keep our heritage alive.

Yonsei, the fourth generation, like the Sansei, may not fully understand the Issei’s struggles and culture. I hope they learn about their roots and maintain their Japanese identity even as they live in America. Passing down these stories is important for our community’s future.

The Issei, or first generation, endured many hardships coming from Japan to America. They preserved Japanese traditions and worked hard to establish our community. I think it is vital that younger generations respect and remember the Issei’s sacrifices and culture.

Mr. Kinya Noguchi once said that we should get together regardless of nationality to build a more familiar feeling with America. I agree that unity among different races is important in this country. His words reflect a hope for harmony and understanding in our diverse society.

It is said that soon Brazil will have a president of Japanese descent, which is a proud moment for people like me. I come from Kagoshima, and I know others from there like Tsuchimoshi Kaikyoshi. This shows how Japanese culture and people have spread and succeeded worldwide.

I am from Kagoshima, Japan, a place known for people like Tsuchimoshi Kaikyoshi. Kagoshima is part of my identity and heritage, and I feel connected to others from there even living abroad. It reminds me of the roots we must not forget.
Tsuchimoshi Kaikyoshi is a person from Kagoshima, like me. Knowing him connects me to my hometown and its community. It is important to remember such figures as part of our cultural heritage.

During the war, I was interned at Tule Lake, where I learned to play the shamisen and worked washing dishes. Life in the camp was hard, but food was secured for children, which was a relief. I sometimes wonder if the camp was better or worse for us, but it was a significant part of my experience.

At Tule Lake, there was a man from San Jose who worked in the kitchen with me. He later passed away, but he left a poem about leaving the camp that I remember well. San Jose is part of the network of places connected to our internment experience.

My daughter graduated from the University of California and is now teaching at a college. She was born and raised in America, so sometimes her way of thinking differs from mine. Her education is a source of pride for our family and shows the progress we have made.

At Tule Lake, I learned to play the shamisen and gave up other arts like flower arrangement. Playing the shamisen was a way to keep connected to my culture during the difficult camp days. It was one of the few comforts I had while interned.
